title: États-Unis ou Europe : qui va mieux ?
gist: Most people assume Europe's economy is falling behind the US, especially in tech. But Americans live shorter, more violent lives, with far more people in prison — and once you account for real purchasing power, Europe isn't actually poorer, just weaker geopolitically. The argument (from an FT column summarising economist Paul Krugman) is that Europe's real vulnerability is security and defense, not economics.

body.fr: Beaucoup de gens pensent que l'économie de l'Europe {{est en déclin|is declining}}.

bd.meaning: Many people think Europe's economy is declining.
chunks: many people (beaucoup de = a lot of)
think that — pensent from penser, the ils/elles form
bd.key: “penser que” + a full sentence. Use penser que to report what people think — the clause after it works like any other sentence.

body.fr: Ils disent que les États-Unis sont {{plus forts|stronger}}, surtout en technologie.

bd.meaning: They say the US is stronger, especially in technology.
chunks: the United States is/are (États-Unis takes a plural verb)
bd.key: plus + adjective = “more ___ / -er.” plus forts = “stronger” — the everyday way to build a comparative.

body.fr: Les États-Unis sont un seul pays, donc ils peuvent {{agir vite|act fast}}.

bd.meaning: The US is a single country, so it can act fast.
chunks: Les États-Unis sont un seul pays
the US is a single/one country
act fast — plain verb after peuvent
bd.key: pouvoir + plain verb. peuvent agir = “can act” — only peuvent changes; agir stays plain.

body.fr: Mais la vie aux États-Unis n'est pas parfaite.

bd.meaning: But life in the US is not perfect.
chunks: life in the US (aux = à + les)
is not perfect — the ne…pas sandwich around est
bd.key: ne … pas around “est.” n'est pas = “is not” — the same sandwich you'll meet again and again.

body.fr: Les Américains vivent {{environ 79 ans|about 79 years}} ; les Européens, environ 83 ans.

bd.meaning: Americans live about 79 years; Europeans, about 83.
chunks: Europeans (same verb reused, dropped after the semicolon)
bd.key: environ = “about / around.” Handy for any approximate number — environ 79 ans = “about 79 years.”

body.fr: Le {{taux de prisonniers|rate of people in prison}} est presque {{huit fois plus élevé|eight times higher}} aux États-Unis qu'en Allemagne.

bd.meaning: The rate of people in prison is almost eight times higher in the US than in Germany.
chunks: the rate of people in prison (taux = rate)
est presque huit fois plus élevé
is almost eight times higher (fois = times)
bd.key: “fois” for multiples. huit fois plus élevé = “eight times higher” — combine a number with fois to say “X times more.”

body.fr: Pourtant, les États-Unis {{dépensent|spend}} plus d'argent pour la santé.

bd.meaning: Yet the US spends more money on health.
bd.key: plus de/d' + noun = “more ___.” Same pattern as before — de becomes d' before a vowel.

body.fr: Depuis 2000, {{le revenu par personne|income per person}} augmente plus vite aux États-Unis.

bd.meaning: Since 2000, income per person has grown faster in the US.
bd.key: depuis + present tense = “since ___, has been ___ing.” French keeps the present where English switches tense: depuis 2000, ça augmente = “since 2000, it's been growing.”

body.fr: Mais c'est étrange : le niveau de vie en Europe {{se rapproche|gets closer}} quand même de celui des États-Unis.

bd.meaning: But here's the strange part: Europe's standard of living is getting closer to the US's anyway.
chunks: but it's strange / here's the odd part
le niveau de vie en Europe
the standard of living in Europe
gets closer (se rapprocher = to get closer)
quand même de celui des États-Unis
to that of the US anyway (celui = “the one” — stands in for “niveau de vie”)
bd.key: “celui” replaces a noun to avoid repeating it: celui des États-Unis = “that of the US,” instead of saying “le niveau de vie des États-Unis” again.

body.fr: Les économistes appellent ça {{un paradoxe|a paradox}}.

bd.meaning: Economists call this a paradox.
chunks: a paradox — something that seems impossible but is true
bd.key: appeler + ça + noun = “call this ___.” Same shape as on appelle ça…, just with a different subject.

body.fr: La raison principale, c'est la technologie.

bd.meaning: The main reason is technology.
bd.key: c'est to name the answer. c'est la technologie = “it's technology” — a simple way to state a cause.

body.fr: Le secteur technologique est beaucoup plus grand aux États-Unis qu'en Europe.

bd.meaning: The tech sector is much bigger in the US than in Europe.
chunks: is much bigger (beaucoup plus = much more)
than in Europe (que → qu' before a vowel)
bd.key: beaucoup plus + adjective = “much more / much ___-er.” beaucoup strengthens the comparative you already know.

body.fr: Cette différence explique presque toute la différence de croissance entre les deux régions.

bd.meaning: This one difference explains almost all of the growth gap between the two regions.
chunks: almost all of (toute agrees with the feminine “différence”)
bd.key: “toute” agrees with the noun it describes. toute la différence is feminine singular, matching différence.

body.fr: En plus, il est difficile de {{mesurer|measure}} la vraie valeur de la technologie.

bd.meaning: Also, it's hard to measure the real value of technology.
chunks: to measure — plain verb after “difficile de”
la vraie valeur de la technologie
the real value of technology (vraie comes before the noun)
bd.key: il est difficile de + plain verb = “it's hard to ___.” Like pour + verb, the verb after de stays plain.

body.fr: Si on compare {{le vrai pouvoir d'achat|real purchasing power}}, l'Europe n'est pas vraiment en retard.

bd.meaning: If you compare real purchasing power, Europe isn't really behind.
chunks: real purchasing power — what money can actually buy
bd.key: “on” again for the general “you.” si on compare = “if you compare” — no need for a specific subject.

body.fr: Les Européens peuvent acheter presque autant de choses que les Américains.

bd.meaning: Europeans can buy almost as many things as Americans.
chunks: almost as many things (autant de = as much/many)
bd.key: autant de ___ que = “as much/many ___ as.” A third comparison shape, alongside plus … que and moins … que.

body.fr: Il y a une raison à ce paradoxe : la technologie américaine aide le monde entier.

bd.meaning: There's a reason for this paradox: American technology helps the whole world.
bd.key: il y a, once more. Same fixed phrase as before, now introducing an explanation instead of a fact.

body.fr: Même les pays qui n'ont pas de grandes entreprises tech {{en profitent|benefit from it}}.

bd.meaning: Even countries that don't have big tech companies benefit from it.
chunks: qui n'ont pas de grandes entreprises tech
that don't have big tech companies (qui = that/which; ne…pas + de after the negation)
bd.key: “qui” + ne…pas together. qui n'ont pas de = “that don't have” — the relative clause and the negation sandwich stack up.

body.fr: Donc, la vie n'est pas {{pire|worse}} en Europe qu'aux États-Unis.

bd.meaning: So, life isn't worse in Europe than in the US.
chunks: in Europe than in the US
bd.key: pire = the irregular “worse.” It doesn't follow the plus/moins pattern — it's its own word, like English “worse” instead of “more bad.”

body.fr: Mais l'Europe reste plus faible, parce qu'elle {{dépend de|depends on}} la technologie américaine.

bd.meaning: But Europe remains weaker, because it depends on American technology.
chunks: because it depends on (que → qu' before a vowel)
bd.key: dépendre de = “to depend on.” A fixed pairing — in French the verb always takes de before what you depend on.

body.fr: Le vrai problème de l'Europe, ce n'est pas l'économie : c'est la sécurité et la défense.

bd.meaning: Europe's real problem isn't the economy: it's security and defense.
chunks: isn't the economy (ce n'est pas = the negative of c'est)
c'est la sécurité et la défense
bd.key: c'est / ce n'est pas, to close. The story ends on the same set phrase it used to open explanations — naming what something is, and what it isn't.

grammar.fr: plus forts / moins longtemps / autant de choses que
grammar.en: Three ways to compare: plus … que, moins … que, autant de … que. “More than,” “less than,” “as much/many as” — this story uses all three.
depuis + present tense = an action that started in the past and is still going. French uses the present here where English reaches for “has been happening” — no extra tense needed.

grammar.fr: Il y a plus de violence / Il y a une raison
grammar.en: il y a = “there is/are.” A fixed phrase, unchanged whether one thing or many follow — it turns up twice in this story.

quiz.q: Qu'est-ce qui explique presque tout le paradoxe entre les États-Unis et l'Europe ?
quiz.a: Le secteur technologique, beaucoup plus grand aux États-Unis, explique presque toute la différence. (The tech sector, much bigger in the US, explains almost the whole gap.)

flag: This compresses a Martin Wolf FT opinion column that itself summarises economist Paul Krugman's argument — it's one economist's interpretation, not settled consensus, though I kept the retelling neutral rather than argumentative. I dropped the named sources (Krugman, Seth Ackerman, the Draghi report) and the detailed GDP-measurement debate (hedonic price adjustments, growth-vs-level accounting) the original leans on heavily. The life-expectancy figure combines men's and women's averages, rounded; the prison figure (US vs Germany) is calculated from the article's per-100,000 numbers. le vrai pouvoir d'achat stands in for the technical term parité de pouvoir d'achat (purchasing power parity) — a native economist would likely use the fuller term.
